message:
feat(wizard): add beginner-friendly SimplerGuide sections to all wizard pages

Enhance the wizard experience for absolute beginners by adding
comprehensive, collapsible guide sections to each wizard step.
These guides provide detailed explanations, step-by-step instructions,
and helpful tips using the SimplerGuide component system.

apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx:
- Add GuideExplain for "VPS (Virtual Private Server)" concept
  explaining what a VPS is with apartment rental analogy
- Add provider comparison guide (Hetzner, OVH, Contabo)
- Add step-by-step signup walkthrough for Hetzner
- Include payment verification explanation and tips
- Add GuideCaution about verification timing expectations

apps/web/app/wizard/create-vps/page.tsx:
- Add GuideExplain for "IP Address" concept with phone number analogy
- Add detailed steps for VPS creation process
- Include provider-specific UI guidance (Hetzner, OVH, Contabo)
- Explain Ubuntu OS selection and LTS meaning
- Guide for adding SSH key during VPS setup
- Tips for server naming and waiting for provisioning

apps/web/app/wizard/ssh-connect/page.tsx:
- Add GuideExplain for "SSH (Secure Shell)" concept
- Add 6-step connection guide with keyboard shortcuts
  (platform-specific paste commands for Mac/Windows)
- Explain the "authenticity of host" fingerprint prompt
- Add "Understanding What You See" section for terminal prompts
- Include common troubleshooting tips

apps/web/app/wizard/run-installer/page.tsx:
- Add GuideExplain for what the install command does
- Explain "idempotent" concept in beginner-friendly terms
- Add step-by-step guide for running the installer
- Explain what gets installed (zsh, AI agents, dev tools)
- Add GuideCaution about not closing terminal during install
- Include timing expectations (10-15 minutes)

apps/web/app/wizard/reconnect-ubuntu/page.tsx:
- Add GuideExplain for why reconnecting as ubuntu matters
  (safety and better experience)
- Add prompt identification guide (root # vs ubuntu $)
- Add 3-step guide for switching users
- Include GuideTip for users who can skip this step

All pages use consistent SimplerGuide component imports:
- SimplerGuide: Collapsible container for guides
- GuideSection: Titled subsection
- GuideStep: Numbered instruction step
- GuideExplain: Term explanation with beginner context
- GuideTip: Helpful tip callout
- GuideCaution: Warning or important note

These additions significantly improve the experience for users
who are new to VPS management, SSH, and command-line tools,
while keeping the interface clean for experienced users who
can collapse or skip the guide sections.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -9,6 +9,14 @@ import { Checkbox } from "@/components/ui/checkbox"; import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; import { useVPSIP, isValidIP } from "@/lib/userPreferences"; +import { + SimplerGuide, + GuideSection, + GuideStep, + GuideExplain, + GuideTip, + GuideCaution, +} from "@/components/simpler-guide"; const CHECKLIST_ITEMS = [ { id: "ubuntu", label: "Selected Ubuntu 25.x (or newest Ubuntu available)" }, @@ -215,6 +223,108 @@ export default function CreateVPSPage() { ))} + {/* Beginner Guide */} + +
+ + An IP address is like a phone number for computers. It's a series + of numbers (like 192.168.1.100) that identifies your VPS on the internet. +

+ You'll need this address to connect to your VPS from your computer. + It's like knowing someone's phone number so you can call them. + + + +
+ + Go to the website where you created your account (Hetzner, OVH, or Contabo) + and sign in with the email and password you created earlier. + + + + Look for a button that says something like: +
    +
  • Hetzner: Click "Add Server" (big blue button)
    • +
  • OVH: Click "Create an instance" or "Order"
    • +
  • Contabo: Go to "Your services" → click the VPS you ordered
    • +
+ + + + Pick a data center close to you for faster speeds: +
    +
  • USA: Choose US-West or US-East
    • +
  • Europe: Choose Germany (FSN) or Finland (HEL)
    • +
  • If unsure, any location works fine!
    • +
+ + + + You'll see a list of "images" or "operating systems". +

+ Look for: Ubuntu 25.04 or Ubuntu 24.04 LTS +
+ + LTS stands for "Long Term Support" — it means the version + is stable and well-supported. + + + + + This is VERY important! Look for a section called "SSH Keys" or + "Authentication". +
    +
  • Click "Add SSH Key" or "Add Key"
    • +
  • Give it a name like "My Laptop" or "ACFS Key"
    • +
  • Paste your public key (the text you copied earlier that starts with ssh-ed25519)
    • +
  • Click Save or Add
    • +
+ + + + Look for a plan with: +
    +
  • 4-8 vCPU (virtual CPUs)
    • +
  • 8-16 GB RAM (memory)
    • +
  • 100GB+ storage
    • +
  • Cost should be around $30-60/month
    • +
+ + + + Click the "Create", "Deploy", or "Order" button. +

+ Your VPS will take 1-5 minutes to start up. You'll see a status like + "Running" or a green indicator when it's ready. + + + + Once your VPS is running, look for the IP address. It's usually shown: +
    +
  • On the main server overview page
    • +
  • In a "Network" or "IP Addresses" section
    • +
  • It looks like: 123.45.67.89
    • +
+
+ Copy this number — you'll paste it in the box below! + +
+ + + + The IP address should be 4 groups of numbers separated by periods, + like 192.168.1.100. + Don't include any letters or extra characters! + + + + Make sure you added your SSH key! If you skip this step, + you won't be able to connect to your VPS. Go back and add it before + continuing. + +
+ + {/* IP Address input */}

Your VPS IP address

@@ -8,6 +8,13 @@ import { CommandCard } from "@/components/command-card"; import { OutputPreview } from "@/components/alert-card"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; import { useVPSIP, useMounted } from "@/lib/userPreferences"; +import { + SimplerGuide, + GuideSection, + GuideStep, + GuideExplain, + GuideTip, +} from "@/components/simpler-guide"; export default function ReconnectUbuntuPage() { const router = useRouter(); @@ -126,6 +133,61 @@ export default function ReconnectUbuntuPage() { + {/* Beginner Guide */} + +
+ + During installation, you may have connected as "root" — the super-admin + account. Now we want you to use the "ubuntu" account instead because: +

+ 1. Safety — The root account can accidentally break things. + The ubuntu account is safer for everyday use. +

+ 2. Better experience — The installer set up special features + (like the colorful prompt) for the ubuntu user. + + + +

Look at your terminal prompt:

+
    +
  • + root@vps:~# + — You're logged in as root (note the # symbol) +
    • +
  • + ubuntu@vps:~$ + — You're logged in as ubuntu (note the $ symbol) +
    • +
+ + + +
+ + Type exit + and press Enter. This closes your connection to the VPS. + + + + Copy and paste the SSH command shown above (the one with{" "} + ubuntu@) + and press Enter. + + + + Your prompt should now show "ubuntu@" at the beginning. + You might also see a fancy colorful prompt! + +
+ + + + If you were already connected as ubuntu (skip button above applies to you), + just click "Skip" or "Continue" — you don't need to do anything! + +
+ + {/* Continue button */}

@@ -7,6 +7,14 @@ import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button"; import { AlertCard } from "@/components/alert-card"; import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; +import { + SimplerGuide, + GuideSection, + GuideStep, + GuideExplain, + GuideTip, + GuideCaution, +} from "@/components/simpler-guide"; interface ProviderInfo { id: string; @@ -223,6 +231,120 @@ export default function RentVPSPage() { you can add your SSH public key during setup. + {/* Beginner Guide */} + +
+ + A VPS is like renting a computer that lives in a data center somewhere + in the world. It runs 24/7, even when your laptop is closed. +

+ Think of it like renting an apartment: you don't own the building, + but you have your own private space to use however you want. +

+ Why do you need one? +
+ AI coding assistants work best on a dedicated server that's always on. + Running them on your laptop would drain your battery and slow everything down. + With a VPS, your AI assistants can work even when you're asleep! + + + +

+ All three providers we recommend are good choices. Here's a simple guide: +

+
    +
  • + Hetzner — Best for most people. Clean interface, fair prices, + popular with developers. We recommend this if you're unsure. +
    • +
  • + OVH — Great if you're in Europe or want European data centers. + Slightly more complex signup process. +
    • +
  • + Contabo — Cheapest option for high specs. Good if budget is + a concern, but their interface is a bit dated. +
    • +
+ + + +
+ + Click on "Hetzner" above, or go to{" "} + + hetzner.com/cloud + + + + + Click "Sign up" or "Register". You'll need: +
    +
  • An email address
    • +
  • A password (make it strong!)
    • +
  • Your name and address
    • +
+ + + + Check your email for a verification link. Click it to confirm + your account. Check your spam folder if you don't see it! + + + + Most providers require a credit card or PayPal. This is normal — + you'll only be charged for what you use. +

+ Expected cost: Around $30-60/month depending on + the plan you choose. You can cancel anytime. + + + + Once your account is verified and payment is set up, you're ready + for the next step where we'll actually create the VPS. + +
+ + + +

+ When choosing a plan, you'll see terms like vCPU, RAM, and SSD. + Here's what they mean: +

+
    +
  • + vCPU (4-8) — The "brain" of the computer. More = faster. + 4 is minimum, 8 is comfortable. +
    • +
  • + RAM (8-16 GB) — Short-term memory. More = can do more things + at once. 8GB minimum, 16GB is better. +
    • +
  • + Storage (100GB+ SSD) — Long-term storage for files and programs. + SSD means it's fast. 100GB is plenty to start. +
    • +
  • + Ubuntu — The operating system we'll install. It's like + Windows or macOS, but for servers. It's free and widely used. +
    • +
+ + + + Don't overthink the provider choice! All three work great. Pick one, + sign up, and you can always switch later if needed. The setup process + is similar for all of them. + + + + Keep your account credentials safe! Write down your + login email and password somewhere secure. You'll need them to + manage your VPS later. + +
+ + {/* Continue button */}

@@ -13,6 +13,14 @@ import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button"; import { CommandCard } from "@/components/command-card"; import { AlertCard, OutputPreview, DetailsSection } from "@/components/alert-card"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; +import { + SimplerGuide, + GuideSection, + GuideStep, + GuideExplain, + GuideTip, + GuideCaution, +} from "@/components/simpler-guide"; const ACFS_COMMAND = `curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/agentic_coding_flywheel_setup/main/install.sh?$(date +%s)" | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe`; @@ -145,6 +153,90 @@ export default function RunInstallerPage() {

+ {/* Beginner Guide */} + +
+ + This command downloads and runs a setup script that automatically installs + everything you need on your VPS. Think of it like running an installer + on your computer — but this one installs dozens of tools at once! +

+ The script is "idempotent" which means it's safe to run multiple times. + If something fails, you can just run it again. + + + +
+ + Your terminal should show something like{" "} + ubuntu@vps:~$ + or root@vps:~#. +

+ If it shows your regular computer name, you need to SSH in first! + + + + Click the copy button on the purple command box above. The command + is quite long — make sure you copy the whole thing! + + + + In your SSH terminal (where you're connected to the VPS), paste + the command and press Enter. +

+ You'll see lots of text scrolling by — this is normal! + + + + The installation takes time because it's downloading and installing + many tools. You'll see progress messages like: +
    +
  • [1/8] Installing zsh...
    • +
  • [2/8] Installing bun...
    • +
  • etc.
    • +
+
+ Don't close the terminal! Let it run until you see + "Installation complete". + +
+ + + +

+ The installer sets up a complete development environment including: +

+
    +
  • + Modern shell (zsh) — A better terminal experience with + colors and suggestions +
    • +
  • + Programming languages — JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, + Rust, and Go +
    • +
  • + AI coding assistants — Claude Code, Codex, and Gemini CLI +
    • +
  • + Developer tools — Git interface, file searchers, and more +
    • +
+ + + + If your internet connection drops during installation, just SSH back in + and run the command again. The installer will pick up where it left off! + + + + Don't close the terminal window while the installation + is running. If you accidentally close it, SSH back in and run the + command again — it will resume from where it stopped. + +
+ + {/* Continue button */}

@@ -9,6 +9,14 @@ import { AlertCard, OutputPreview } from "@/components/alert-card"; import { cn } from "@/lib/utils"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; import { useVPSIP, useUserOS, useMounted } from "@/lib/userPreferences"; +import { + SimplerGuide, + GuideSection, + GuideStep, + GuideExplain, + GuideTip, + GuideCaution, +} from "@/components/simpler-guide"; interface TroubleshootingItem { error: string; @@ -250,6 +258,100 @@ export default function SSHConnectPage() { + {/* Beginner Guide */} + +
+ + SSH is a way to securely connect to another computer over the internet. + It's like making a phone call to your VPS — once connected, everything + you type appears on the VPS, not your local computer. +

+ When you "SSH into" a computer, you're essentially remote-controlling it + through text commands. + + + +
+ + Open the terminal app you installed earlier (Ghostty, WezTerm, or Windows Terminal). + + + + Look at the gray command box above. Click the copy button + on the right side (it looks like two overlapping squares). + + + + Click inside your terminal window, then paste: +
    +
  • Mac: ⌘ + V
    • +
  • Windows: Right-click inside the terminal, or Ctrl + V
    • +
+ + + + Press the Enter key to run the command. + + + + You'll see a scary-looking message about "authenticity of host" + and a "fingerprint". This is normal for first-time connections! +

+ Type yes (spelled out, not just "y") + and press Enter. + + + + If successful, you'll see a new prompt like: + + ubuntu@vps:~$ + + The "ubuntu@vps" part means you're now controlling the VPS! + Everything you type from now on runs on the VPS, not your laptop. + +
+ + + +

+ After connecting, your terminal looks different because you're now + "inside" the VPS: +

+
    +
  • + ubuntu@ + — This is your username on the VPS +
    • +
  • + vps + — This is the VPS hostname (might be different) +
    • +
  • + ~ + — This means you're in your "home" folder +
    • +
  • + $ + — This means the terminal is ready for your next command +
    • +
+ + + + To disconnect from the VPS and return to your local computer, type{" "} + exit + and press Enter. You can always reconnect using the same SSH command. + + + + "Permission denied" error? This usually means your SSH key + wasn't added to the VPS correctly. Go back to your VPS provider's control + panel and make sure your public key is added. Then try the "root" command + shown above as a fallback. + +
+ + {/* Continue button */}
